ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for white miso paste; SNOMED CT coverage of fermented condiments is limited.

allergens: gluten (rice koji may contain trace gluten; traditional white miso uses rice koji which is gluten-free, but cross-contamination risk exists)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of white miso paste is approximately 1 tablespoon (17 g), commonly dissolved in hot water to make miso soup or used as a marinade or glaze base.

nutrient_source.title: Miso, white — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for white miso paste (F10060048). All core macros and secondary nutrients are as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for miso (NDB No. 16112) as 1.1 g/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for white miso paste was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 33 is estimated based on the published GI of fermented soybean products and soy-based foods (GI range 20–40, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (33 × 3.72) / 100 ≈ 1.2, where carbohydrate per 17 g serving = 21.9 × 0.17 = 3.72 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 4900 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: White miso paste contains 4900 mg sodium per 100 g, far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold; individuals with hypertension should restrict use to no more than 1 teaspoon (approximately 5–8 g) per serving and account for miso sodium within their total daily sodium budget of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic impact per serving despite moderate per-100g sugar; fermentation improves insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although white miso contains 11.9 g sugar and 21.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, typical serving sizes of 5–17 g result in negligible glycaemic load (GL ≈ 1.2 per tablespoon); fermented soy isoflavones and bioactive peptides have evidence for improving insulin sensitivity, making moderate use appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium is a cardiovascular risk factor; soy isoflavones and fermentation offer cardioprotective benefits
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The very high sodium density (4900 mg/100 g) poses a cardiovascular risk if consumed in large quantities; however, soy isoflavones and fermentation-derived bioactive peptides in white miso have demonstrated modest LDL-lowering and blood pressure-modulating effects in peer-reviewed studies, so small-quantity use (1–2 tsp per day) may be acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fermented food with anti-inflammatory soy isoflavones; high sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: White miso supports gut microbiome health and provides anti-inflammatory soy isoflavones relevant to RA management, but its very high sodium content (4900 mg/100 g) may exacerbate RA-related tissue oedema; use in small quantities and choose low-sodium varieties where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White miso paste (shiro miso, 白味噌) is a traditional Japanese fermented condiment produced by fermenting soybeans with rice koji (Aspergillus oryzae cultures) and salt for a shorter period than darker miso varieties, typically 1–3 months. The result is a thick, pale cream-to-yellow paste with a mild, slightly sweet, and subtly umami flavour profile, distinguishing it from the more pungent red or mixed miso varieties. Per 100 g, white miso provides 192 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 6.0 g fat, 21.9 g carbohydrate (11.9 g sugars), 0.6 g dietary fibre, and a very high sodium content of 4900 mg. It is rich in iron (4.0 mg/100 g) and calcium (100 mg/100 g). Despite the high per-100g sodium and sugar figures, white miso is used as a condiment in small quantities — typically 5–17 g per serving — making the actual per-serving nutrient contribution modest. Clinically, the high sodium density is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. For type 2 diabetes, the low glycaemic load per serving and evidence that fermented soy foods improve insulin sensitivity make moderate use appropriate. White miso is a staple of Japanese cuisine, used in miso soup, salad dressings, marinades, glazes, and ramen broths. It is plant-based, dairy-free, and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. The fermentation process produces live cultures, bioactive peptides, and soy isoflavones with documented anti-inflammatory and gut-health benefits.

meal_context: miso glaze for fish or vegetables

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (F10060048) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central miso entry (NDB 16112) as the closest approved-source equivalent. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008) as no direct white miso GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central miso entry (NDB No. 16112) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for white miso paste are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (F10060048) or confirmed in USDA FoodData Central for this specific white miso variant as of 2026. 3. GI of 33 is an analogous estimate based on fermented soybean product GI range (20–40) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct white miso GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; traditional miso fermentation does not use animal-derived ingredients but halal certification varies by producer. 5. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed zero per HPB source; white miso is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived cholesterol.
